title: Bread sauce
subtitle: Clove-infused milk thickened with white breadcrumbs
excerpt: Whole milk infused with onion, cloves, and bay, then thickened with white breadcrumbs and finished with butter. The essential accompaniment to roast chicken, turkey, and game birds in the British tradition. Made from stale white bread, not breadcrumbs from a bag — texture and absorption are different.
sourceNotes: Bread sauce is one of the oldest British sauces, predating flour-thickened sauces by several centuries. Using bread to thicken sauces was standard in medieval European cooking; bread sauce survived in Britain as a specific pairing with poultry and game while most other bread-thickened preparations disappeared. It is entirely absent from French cuisine and is one of the distinctly British elements of the roast dinner tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 days ahead, refrigerate, and reheat gently with a splash of milk to loosen.

content.text: The flavour of bread sauce comes entirely from the infused milk. The longer the onion and cloves steep, the more flavour the sauce will have. Leave it for at least 20 minutes; 1 hour is better. Do not rush the infusion.

Use day-old white bread with the crusts removed, torn into rough pieces. Fresh bread will not absorb as well and produces a gluey texture. The sauce should be thick enough to hold its shape on the plate but not stiff — adjust with milk after the bread has absorbed.

items.prepNote: day-old, crusts removed, torn into pieces
white pepper if preferred for colour

content.text: Push the {{cloves}} into the cut surfaces of the {{onion}}. Place in a saucepan with the {{whole-milk}} and {{bay-leaves}}. Heat gently until just below a simmer. Remove from the heat and leave to infuse for at least 20 minutes. Fish out and discard the onion and bay leaves. Return the pan to low heat. Add the {{white-bread}} pieces and stir gently. Cook over the lowest heat for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the bread has absorbed the milk and the sauce is thick. Add the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Stir until the butter has melted. If the sauce is too thick, add a splash of warm milk and stir. Serve warm alongside roast chicken, turkey, or game.

items.symptom: Sauce gluey and stodgy rather than smooth and creamy
items.cause: Too much bread, or bread not properly stale
items.fix: Start with slightly less bread and add more as needed. The bread should melt into the sauce, not form lumps. Fresh bread produces a different, less pleasant texture

items.cause: Insufficient infusion time, or too few cloves
items.fix: The cloves are essential — without them the sauce tastes of nothing. Increase to 6 cloves if your taste runs to a more pronounced flavour, and extend the infusion to an hour

items.symptom: Sauce seized and too thick after reheating
items.cause: Normal behaviour — bread-thickened sauces thicken further as they cool
items.fix: Add warm milk a tablespoon at a time, stirring over gentle heat. Never boil bread sauce — it will scorch on the bottom

content.text: Bread sauce is the accompaniment that non-British people find most strange and British people find most essential at a Christmas table. It is paired with roast chicken, turkey, pheasant, and partridge. Its absence from a Christmas dinner is noted. The flavour is subtle (onion, clove, and milk) and its role is textural as much as flavour: a soft, mild counterpoint to rich roast meat and sharp cranberry sauce.
